title: Event-Driven Backbone
sysPivotQ: Which delivery guarantee?

sysRaw: {"intro":"Zoom out to where the backbone sits between a producer and its consumers.","stages":[],"pivots":[{"q":"Which delivery guarantee?","chip":"→ at-least-once plus an idempotent consumer, never exactly-once delivery\nThe broker retries until acked, so it never drops but may duplicate. Exactly-once delivery isn't achievable across a network; at-least-once plus a dedup check gives effectively-o
modelRaw: {"selectors":["the delivery guarantee","the idempotent consumer"],"answers":[{"opener":"Why it is at-least-once","sub":"The line that pre-empts the exactly-once trap.","beats":[{"l":"At-least-once, never dropped","c":"key","t":"the broker retries until acked"},{"l":"Duplicates from the ack gap","c":"store","t":"crash or lost ack, broker can’t tell"},{"l":"Effectively-once via dedup","c":"note","t":"idempotent consumer closes it"}]},{"opener":"Making duplicates harmless","sub":"The point most answers skip.","beats":[{"l":"Stable id per message","c":"key","t":"assigned at emit, never on retry"},{"l":"Atomic claim with the work","c":"store","t":"conditional insert, not read-then-write"},{"l":"Skip a seen id","c":"note","t":"ack with no second effect"}]}]}

sysRaw: {"intro":"Notifications are the delivery boundary. The systems around it produce events; notifications fan them out to the channels a user watches, once; the user sees one. Knowing what sits on either side — and being able to walk the whole chain from an emitted event to a seen notification — is what turns a ‘send an email’ answer into a systems
wbFoot: The one people forget: step 8. A good system doesn’t fire every channel every time — it sends the cheap in-app first and escalates to email only if unseen, canceling the pending email if the user opens the in-app. If you send both at once, the interviewer knows you’ve built a for-loop over channels, not a considerate notification system.

sysRaw: {"intro":"Zoom out: the attribute store is the boundary between a fixed schema and the per-device config that changes faster than migrations can keep up. Upstream, product needs new settings constantly; downstream, 50,000 devices each resolve their own config. The store absorbs that variability as data — definitions and overrides — so the schema stays stable while config
wbFoot: The one people forget: the pivot. Reconstructing an entity from row-per-attribute storage is the EAV tax — done per-attribute it’s the N+1 that sinks the design. Fetch all the value rows in one query and pivot once, and know that constant whole-entity reconstruction is the signal to cache or to promote hot attributes to columns.
